title: Harnessing DEN models for quantum computing tasks on neutral atom QPUs
authors: Chiara Vercellino, Giacomo Vitali, Paolo Viviani, Alberto Scionti, Olivier Terzo, Bartolomeo Montrucchio
abstract: We present our work on effectively representing unit-disk graphs on the registers of neutral atom quantum machines. Specifically, we aimed to embed graphs corresponding to proteins and cellular antenna networks into unit-disk graphs, ensuring compatibility with the registers of two real QPUs: Orion Alpha by PASQAL and Aquila by QuEra. To address machine-specific constraints, we made adjustments and integrated Distance Encoder Networks (DEN) from our previous work. Despite these challenges, we successfully embedded up to 76% of protein-representing graphs for a quantum machine learning classification task on the Aquila QPU, and all subgraphs derived from 90 antenna geographical positions in Turin, Italy, on the Orion Alpha QPU. In the latter case, the graphs represented instances of the graph coloring problem, which we tackled using the hybrid quantum-classical algorithm BBQ-mIS. These promising results underscore the effectiveness and versatility of our embedding approach for representing unit-disk graphs on neutral atom quantum computers across diverse applications.
summary: The researchers developed a neural-network-based method called Distance Encoder Networks to map complex graphs onto the physical constraints of neutral atom quantum computers. They successfully applied this to solve graph coloring for antenna networks and protein classification tasks. The approach is much faster than traditional optimization methods, though its success rate decreases as the number of atoms increases.
main_problem: Developing efficient methods to embed Unit-Disk Graphs (UDGs) onto the physical registers of real-world neutral atom QPUs while satisfying hardware-specific constraints like fixed lattices and row-spacing.
main_result: The Distance Encoder Networks (DEN) approach successfully embedded antenna network graphs on the Orion Alpha QPU and achieved up to 76% success for protein classification tasks on the Aquila QPU, significantly outperforming traditional optimization solvers in speed.
method: A neural-network-based optimization solver (DEN) using an Embedding Loss Function (ELF) to learn feasible embeddings, adapted with specific penalty terms for hardware constraints like row-spacing and lattice mapping.
model_or_system: Neutral atom quantum processing units (QPUs) using Rubidium-87 atoms, specifically the QuEra Aquila and PASQAL Orion Alpha platforms, utilizing the Rydberg blockade mechanism to represent graphs.
key_observables: The gap between non-adjacent and adjacent vertex distances (d_non-adj - d_adj), and the feasibility rate of embeddings.
important_parameters: Rydberg blockade radius, atom register dimensions (e.g., 75um x 76um for Aquila), minimum row spacing (4um), and vertex count (n).
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the Rydberg blockade can effectively represent the edges of a Unit-Disk Graph and that the graph can be mapped to the available qubit-based register.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the DEN architecture; Fig 2 illustrates the row-spacing loss component; Fig 4 shows the decline in embedding success rate as vertex count increases; Fig 5 shows the distribution of the distance gap; Fig 6 shows a visual example of a successful large-scale embedding.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of graph embedding on neutral atom hardware, describes the DEN architecture and its modifications for specific QPU constraints (Orion Alpha and Aquila), presents experimental results on antenna networks and protein datasets, and discusses scaling challenges and future replication strategies.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it bridges the gap between abstract graph problems and the physical constraints of Rydberg-based quantum hardware, providing a scalable heuristic for mapping complex networks to real-world atom arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Modified DEN model architecture for feasible embeddings on Aquila QPU - an example of embedding a 5-vertex graph.

Fig. 2: ELFrow component for incorporating the row-spacing constraint. ELFrow equals 0 in cases where two vertices either share the same row or are separated by a row-distance greater than 4µm (squared distance ≥16µm2).
Fig. 3: Mapping of the feasible embedding for sample 19G_1cc to Orion Alpha register.

Fig. 4: Percentage of graph samples for which the DEN model found an Aquila-feasible embedding, grouped by the number of vertices n. The numbers reported in each bin represent the total number of graph samples for each value of n.
Fig. 5: Gap distributions of d¬adj −Dadj grouped by the number of vertices n, for the graph-embedded samples in the PROTEINS_12 and PROTEINS_16 datasets.
